skill.name: fp-ts-errors
skill.description: Guide TypeScript developers in replacing thrown exceptions with fp-ts Either and TaskEither patterns for typed, composable error handling.
skill.summary: Use fp-ts Either and TaskEither to model errors as explicit TypeScript values.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, blocker, and reconnaissance patterns, but context shows they are Markdown code fences, TypeScript examples, and ordinary prose. The only retained risk factor is documentation-only network usage in fetch examples; no prompt injection, secret handling, executable scripts, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown fences and inline TypeScript examples, not shell execution. No evidence found of child process APIs, shell invocation, or command strings executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are fenced TypeScript snippets and a quick-reference table. Semantic review found no executable wrapper or instruction that runs those snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Instructional Fetch Examples Only
low_findings.description: The network findings are fetch calls inside TypeScript teaching examples for TaskEither. They describe application code patterns and do not cause the skill itself to make outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network calls are inside Markdown code examples for wrapping async operations. No surrounding automation, installation script, or hidden endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Only Blocker Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings appear to be keyword matches on words such as errors, includes, find, and id in ordinary TypeScript validation examples. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, host discovery, or system probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed contexts are metadata, form validation, and error handling examples. They do not contain weak hash algorithms, filesystem enumeration, or operating system reconnaissance commands.

content.value_statement: Thrown exceptions hide failure paths from TypeScript and make control flow harder to review. This skill shows how to model failures with Either and TaskEither so callers handle success and error cases explicitly.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to replace try/catch with Either return values.
Shows how to chain failing operations with Either and TaskEither.
Demonstrates collecting multiple validation errors with applicative validation.
Covers conversions from nullable values, throwing functions, promises, and legacy APIs.
Provides examples for API calls, bulk operations, retries, and fallback behavior.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not execute or validate project code.
Examples assume familiarity with TypeScript generics and fp-ts imports.
It does not cover every fp-ts module or advanced typeclass concept.
It does not replace domain-specific security review for API or database code.

use_cases.title: Refactor Exception-Heavy Services
use_cases.description: Convert service functions from hidden thrown errors to typed Either results with explicit caller handling.

use_cases.title: Build Typed Form Validation
use_cases.description: Collect all validation failures at once and return structured field errors for user interfaces.

use_cases.title: Wrap Async Boundaries Safely
use_cases.description: Represent API, database, and cache failures with TaskEither instead of mixed Promise and try/catch flows.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Throwing Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner refactor from exceptions to Either.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me convert this TypeScript function from throwing errors to returning fp-ts Either. Explain the new error type and caller handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design Validation Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an fp-ts validation flow that collects all errors for these fields. Use NonEmptyArray and explain how each field error is returned.

prompt_templates.title: Wrap Async Calls
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor these Promise-based API calls into TaskEither. Include typed error cases for network, parsing, and not-found failures.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced adoption in an existing codebase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module and propose a staged migration to Either and TaskEither. Identify boundaries, shared error types, and risky call sites.

output_examples.input: A user lookup function throws when the id is missing or no user exists.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests returning a typed failure for missing input or missing user, then handling both success and failure at the call site.

output_examples.input: A signup form validates email, password, and age one field at a time.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends applicative validation so all field errors are collected and can be shown together.

output_examples.input: A dashboard loads user data, posts, notifications, and settings with nested try/catch blocks.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines a TaskEither pipeline that wraps each async boundary and keeps final rendering decisions in one fold step.

best_practices: Wrap throwing and Promise-based APIs at system boundaries before composing business logic.
Use explicit domain error types instead of plain strings when the caller needs different recovery paths.
Handle Either and TaskEither values at the edge with fold, pattern matching, or a clear adapter.
anti_patterns: Do not mix unchecked throws inside functions that promise Either or TaskEither results.
Do not collapse every error into a generic message when the caller needs context.
Do not use TaskEither for simple synchronous code when Either is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill require fp-ts experience?
faq.answer: Basic TypeScript knowledge is enough, but familiarity with generics and pipe makes the examples easier to apply.

faq.question: Can it help replace try/catch code?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to wrap throwing code with Either or TaskEither and handle failures explicitly.

faq.question: Does it run code in my repository?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples only. It does not execute tests, install packages, or modify files by itself.

faq.question: Is TaskEither only for HTTP requests?
faq.answer: No. TaskEither can wrap any asynchronous operation that may fail, including database, cache, and queue calls.

faq.question: How does it handle multiple validation errors?
faq.answer: It shows applicative validation with NonEmptyArray so independent checks can return all errors together.

faq.question: Can this pattern work with legacy APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows how to convert nullable values, throwing functions, and promises at application boundaries.
